claim: Metastatic colorectal cancer treated with a single agent fluoropyrimidines resulted in reduced efficacy and lower quality of life when compared with oxaliplatin-based chemotherapy in elderly patients.

result.claim: Metastatic colorectal cancer treated with a single agent fluoropyrimidines resulted in reduced efficacy and lower quality of life when compared with oxaliplatin-based chemotherapy in elderly patients.
result.reasoning: The claim is supported by evidence from the MRC FOCUS2 trial (Paper ID: 15476777), which investigated chemotherapy options in elderly and frail patients with metastatic colorectal cancer. The study concluded that "a combination including oxaliplatin was preferable to single-agent fluoropyrimidines" (prop_2453). While the primary endpoint of progression-free survival (PFS) for oxaliplatin addition was not statistically significant (median 5.8 months with oxaliplatin vs 4.5 months without; HR 0.84, 95% CI 0.69-1.01, p=0.07), the trend favored oxaliplatin, indicating better efficacy (prop_2436, prop_2437). Furthermore, the use of oxaliplatin was predictive of better overall treatment utility (OTU) with an odds ratio of 0.57 (95% CI 0.39-0.82) (prop_2449, prop_2450). Regarding quality of life, the study found that the risk of grade 3 or worse toxic effects was not significantly increased with oxaliplatin (38% with oxaliplatin vs 32% without; p=0.17) (prop_2441, prop_2442, prop_2443). Although the paper doesn't explicitly state "lower quality of life" for fluoropyrimidines, the overall preference for oxaliplatin-based combinations, coupled with the efficacy benefits and non-significant increase in severe toxicity, suggests a better overall profile including quality of life for oxaliplatin-based regimens in this patient population.

evidence.text: The trial involved patients with previously untreated advanced colorectal cancer.
evidence.source: Chemotherapy options in elderly and frail patients with metastatic colorectal cancer (MRC FOCUS2): an open-label, randomised factorial trial

evidence.text: Patients were randomly assigned to one of four groups: 48-h intravenous fluorouracil with levofolinate (group A); oxaliplatin and fluorouracil (group B); capecitabine (group C); or oxaliplatin and capecitabine (group D).
evidence.source: Chemotherapy options in elderly and frail patients with metastatic colorectal cancer (MRC FOCUS2): an open-label, randomised factorial trial

evidence.text: 70 of 221 patients (32%) not receiving oxaliplatin experienced grade 3 or worse toxic effects.
evidence.source: Chemotherapy options in elderly and frail patients with metastatic colorectal cancer (MRC FOCUS2): an open-label, randomised factorial trial

evidence.text: Use of oxaliplatin (0·57, 0·39-0·82) was predictive of better OTU.
evidence.source: Chemotherapy options in elderly and frail patients with metastatic colorectal cancer (MRC FOCUS2): an open-label, randomised factorial trial

evidence.text: Patients were considered unfit for full-dose chemotherapy.

evidence.text: 69 of 124 patients (56%) receiving fluorouracil reported improvement in global QoL.

evidence.text: Compared with extensive metabolizers, those with decreased CYP2D6 activity had a worse disease-free survival (HR, 1.29).
